items.original: Ребята, на его месте должен был быть… я! — Напьёшься — будешь
items.translation:
Guys,
I should be in his place.
Get drunk—you will be!
items.reference: Guys, I should have been in his place! — Get drunk enough, and you will be!
items.justification: The translation misses the past tense of 'должен был быть' (should have been) by using 'I should be', slightly altering the dramatic context of a near-miss.

items.original: Я не трус… но я боюсь
items.translation:
No, I’m not a coward,
but I’m afraid...
items.reference: I'm no coward... but I'm overpowered by fear!
items.justification: The translation is completely literal and flat. It misses the rhythmic, bouncing, or paradoxical comedic effect highlighted in the reference analysis.

items.original: У Вас… ус… отклеился
items.translation: Your... mustache came unstuck...
items.reference: Um... your... mustache... is peeling off.
items.justification: The translation perfectly conveys the absurdly polite and mundane observation of the fake mustache coming off in a tense situation.

items.original: Леди, синьора, фрау, мисс, к сожалению, ничего не выйдет… Руссо туристо! Облико морале! Ферштейн?
items.translation:
You know, signora, frau, miss,
unfortunately, nothing will come of it!
Russo tourist...
Moral character... versteh’n?
items.reference: Lady, signora, frau, miss... unfortunately, nothing's going to happen. Russo Turisto! Obliko Morale! Verstehen?
items.justification: The translation misses 'Леди' (Lady) at the beginning, substituting it with 'You know'. Moreover, translating 'Obliko morale' into standard English ('Moral character') loses the specific pseudo-foreign gibberish flair that makes the original line iconic.

items.original: Шеф, всё пропало, всё пропало! Гипс снимают, клиент уезжает…
items.translation:
Boss, it’s all lost! All lost!!!
They’re removing the cast, and the client is leaving.
items.reference: Boss, everything's lost, everything's lost! The cast is coming off, the client is leaving!
items.justification: The translation accurately captures the transition from abstract panic to the absurdly specific details of the cast and the client. The comedic effect is preserved.

items.original: Как говорит наш любимый шеф: «Если человек идиот, то это надолго»!
items.translation:
As our beloved boss says,
if a человек is an idiot—
it’s for the long haul.
items.reference: As our beloved boss says: 'If a man is an idiot, it's for a long time!'
items.justification: A major accuracy issue occurs because the Russian word 'человек' was left completely untranslated in the English text.

items.original: Дитя́м — мороженое, его бабе — цветы. Смотры, нэ пэрэпутай!.. Кутузоу!
items.translation:
ice cream for the kids,
flowers for his woman.
Don’t mix it up!
Kutuzov!
items.reference: Ice cream for the young'uns, flowers for his broad. Look now, don't mix 'em up! Kutuzov!
items.justification: The translation is too standard. It fails to capture Lyolik's uneducated, gravelly sociolect ('kids' and 'woman' are too neutral), missing a key component of his character's voice.

items.original: Как говорит наш дорогой шеф, за чужой счёт пьют даже трезвенники… и язвенники!
items.translation:
As our dear boss says,
on someone else’s tab even teetotalers drink—
and ulcer sufferers too.
items.reference: On someone else's dime, even teetotalers and convalescers drink!
items.justification: The translation is clunky and entirely abandons the rhythmic, proverbial balance required by the reference analysis (the rhyme of trezvenniki/yazvenniki).

items.original: Шампанское по утрам пьют или аристократы, или дегенераты
items.translation:
Champagne in the morning is drunk
either by aristocrats or degenerates!
items.reference: In the morning, champagne is drunk only by aristocrats and degenerates.
items.justification: The translation successfully communicates the absurd duality of early-morning drinking and maintains the rhyming punchline.

items.original: Достаточно одной таблэтки
items.translation: One tablet is enough...
items.reference: Just one 'tab-LEHT' will do.
items.justification: The translation uses standard English and completely loses the iconic mispronunciation that gives the original line its distinctive, comedic flavor.

items.original: Наши люди в булочную на такси не ездят
items.translation:
Our people
don’t take taxis to the bakery!
items.reference: Our people don't take taxis to the bakery!
items.justification: The translation accurately matches the reference and effectively delivers the absurdly strict moral judgment characteristic of the Soviet era.

items.original: Семё-он Семёныч!
items.translation: Semyon Semyonovich...
items.reference: Semyo-o-on Semyonych!
items.justification: The translation spells out the full formal patronymic ('Semyonovich') rather than the colloquial, weary 'Semyonych', and fails to replicate the elongated vowel that visually and phonetically conveys the character's exasperation.

items.original: Чтоб ты жил… на одну… зарплату!
items.translation:
May you live
on just one
salary!
items.reference: May you live... on nothing but... your salary!
items.justification: The translation captures the essence of the joke—the ultimate underworld curse of having to live on an honest state wage.

items.original: Цигель-цигель, цигель, ай-лю-лю
items.translation: Lunda hanamarun, tsigel, tsigel, tsigel, ai-lyulyu...
items.reference: Zigel-zigel, zigel, ai-lyu-lyu!
items.justification: The translation effectively transliterates the gibberish, preserving the phonetic comicality and urgent rhythm as intended.

items.original: Поскользнулся. Упал. Закрытый перелом. Потерял сознание. Очнулся — гипс
items.translation:
I slipped, I fell...
a closed fracture...
Lost consciousness, came to—
a cast!
items.reference: Slipped. Fell. Closed fracture. Passed out. Woke up in a cast.
items.justification: Adding pronouns ('I slipped, I fell...') breaks the staccato, telegraphic, almost robotic delivery of the original Russian string of verbless/subjectless statements.

items.original: Лёгким движением руки брюки превращаются… Брюки превращаются… Превращаются брюки… В элегантные шорты
items.translation:
With a slight movement of the hand,
the trousers turn into...
the trousers turn into...
the trousers turn...
into elegant shorts!
items.reference: With a light flick of the wrist, the trousers transform... The trousers transform... Transform, the trousers do... into elegant shorts.
items.justification: The translation accurately handles the original phrasing without omitting words, successfully preserving the repetitive stalling caused by the wardrobe malfunction.

items.original: Не виноватая я! Он сам пришёл!
items.translation: It’s not my fault! He came on his own!
items.reference: Twasn't my fault! He came here on his own!
items.justification: The translation uses standard, grammatically correct English ('It’s not my fault!'). It completely fails to reflect the morphological slip and sudden drop into an uneducated register that is central to the joke.

items.original: Кто возьмёт билетов пачку, тот получит… — Водокачку!
items.translation:
Whoever buys a pack of tickets gets...
A pumping station!
items.reference: Buy a whole ticket pack, win... a tractor rack!
items.justification: The original relies heavily on a childish, sing-song rhyme. The translation is literal and loses the rhyme entirely, making the punchline fall flat.

items.original: А если не будут брать - отключим газ!
items.translation: If they won’t take it—we’ll turn off the gas!
items.reference: And if they won't buy them—we'll shut off the gas!
items.justification: The translation effectively conveys the extreme, absurd threat of cutting off utilities for failing to buy lottery tickets, preserving the character's tyrannical nature.

items.original: Ну, будете у нас на Колыме!.. — Нет, уж лучше Вы к нам!
items.translation:
Well, if you’re ever in Kolyma...
No, better you come to us!
items.reference: Well, if you're ever in Kolyma... — No, better you visit us!
items.justification: The translation perfectly captures the polite yet horrified deflection of an invitation to Kolyma, maintaining the dark historical humor.

items.original: В таком виде… я не могу. Мне нужно… сначала… принять вѫнну, выпить чашечку кофэ…
items.translation:
Looking like this...
I can’t.
I must... first...
take a bath,
drink a cup of coffee...
items.reference: In this state... I simply cannot. I must first... bathe... and have a cup of 'coff-ey'...
items.justification: The translation uses the standard word 'coffee', completely missing the affected, pseudo-aristocratic mispronunciation ('kof-e') that is the main comedic point of the line.

items.original: — А у вас нет такого же, но с пелра… с перламутровыми пуговицами? — К сожалению, нет. — Нет? Будем искать…
items.translation:
And do you have the same thing, but
with mother-of-pearl buttons?
Unfortunately, no...
No... we’ll keep looking...
items.reference: — You wouldn't have the same one, but with mother-of-pearl buttons? — Unfortunately, no. — No? Must keep looking then!
items.justification: The translation successfully communicates the stalling tactic of asking for an absurdly specific variant of a mundane item, preserving the scene's situational comedy.

items.original: Нет! На это я пойтить не могу!
items.translation: No, I can’t go along with that!
items.reference: No! I ain't doing no such thing!
items.justification: The translation uses completely standard grammar ('I can't go along with that'), stripping away the rural, folksy grammatical error ('poytit') that gives the character's refusal its specific comedic color.

summary.interpretation: 2.35 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.47 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
